perturbation.original_text: LICENSEE shall ensure that all Licensed Technology distributed by LICENSEE shall be subject to a shrink-wrap agreement or other end user agreement which contains a provision substantially similar to the provision set forth in Exhibit D.
perturbation.changed_text: LICENSEE shall endeavor to ensure that all Licensed Technology distributed by LICENSEE is subject to a shrink-wrap agreement or other end user agreement that generally reflects the spirit of Exhibit D.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall ensure' to 'shall endeavor to ensure' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'contains a provision substantially similar to the provision set forth in Exhibit D' with 'generally reflects the spirit of Exhibit D' introduces significant ambiguity. The original text imposes a relatively clear requirement to mirror Exhibit D, whereas the modified text allows for subjective interpretation. This could allow LICENSEE to distribute Licensed Technology under agreements that do not adequately protect D2's rights, which would happen if the spirit is not upheld to the letter.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the requirement for end-user agreements to substantially mirror Exhibit D, the LICENSEE could potentially distribute the Licensed Technology under terms that do not adequately protect D2's copyright, thereby infringing on D2's exclusive rights as outlined in the Copyright Act.

Exclusive rights in copyrighted works Subject to sections 107 through 122, the owner of copyright under this title has the exclusive rights to do and to authorize any of the following: (1) to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies or phonorecords; (2) to prepare derivative works based upon the copyrighted work; (3) to distribute copies or phonorecords of the copyrighted work to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership, or by rental,
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to protect copyright, potentially infringing on the exclusive rights of the copyright holder. The original text requires a shrink-wrap agreement 'substantially similar' to Exhibit D, providing a clear standard. The changed text, requiring only that the agreement 'generally reflects the spirit of Exhibit D,' introduces ambiguity and weakens copyright protection. This contradicts 17 USC 106, which grants copyright owners exclusive rights, including the right to reproduce and distribute their work. The modified text allows for end-user agreements that may not adequately protect these rights, potentially enabling copyright infringement.

perturbation.original_text: D2 shall promptly correct any Software License D2 Technologies, Inc. and Maintenance Agreement August 4, 1997 CONFIDENTIAL 12 errors in the Licensed Technology, or failures of the Licensed Technology according to the terms of Article 6 of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: D2 shall, when feasible, attempt to correct errors in the Licensed Technology or failures of the Licensed Technology, subject to resource availability and prioritization.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states 'D2 shall promptly correct,' establishing a clear obligation. The modified text introduces ambiguity with 'when feasible, attempt to correct,' 'subject to resource availability,' and 'prioritization.' These phrases weaken the obligation, making it unclear when D2 is actually required to correct errors and allowing them to potentially avoid their responsibility based on subjective factors.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - General Contract Law (New York)
perturbation.law_explanation: By adding subjective conditions ('when feasible', 'resource availability,' 'prioritization') to the obligation to correct errors, the modification introduces an element of uncertainty. If D2 fails to correct errors, it becomes more difficult for LICENSEE to claim breach of contract because D2 can argue that correction was not 'feasible' due to lack of resources or prioritization. This weakens the original contractual obligation, making enforcement against D2 in New York (governing law per 15.1) more difficult and creating a potential contradiction with established contract law principles regarding clear obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: breach of contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you breach of contract A breach of contract occurs whenever a party who entered a contract fails to perform their promised obligations. Due to the frequency of breaches of contract, a robust body of law has grown to resolve the ensuing disputes. The overarching goal of contract law is to place the harmed party in the same economic position they would have been in had no breach of contract occurred. As a result, the default remedy available for a breach of contract is monetary damages . Generally, these damages are limited to what is listed in the contract and, unlike damages from tort cases, courts do not award punitive damages for breaches of contract. For example, if a party agrees to pay $50,000 to have their house painted but is only willing to hand over $10,000 once the painting is complete, the court will award the painters $40,000 in damages. This hesitancy to award punitive damages is due to the theory of efficient breach which argues that breaching contracts and paying damages is sometimes economically beneficial for society as a whole. Nonetheless, in specific circumstances, a party may successfully recover more money than initially contracted for under the doctrine of reliance damages . Under this doctrine , a party who reasonably relied upon a contract that was later breached can be granted compensation for the reasonable expenses they incurred due to that reliance. For example, a party who purchases lifeguard equipment in reliance upon a pool construction contract’s fulfillment may be able to recover the costs of the lifeguard equipment in the event of a breach. Reliance damages are based upon the principle of promissory estoppel , and granting them is subject to the court’s discretion. That said, parties harmed by a breach of contract have a duty to mitigate that harm. For example, before they could recover, the aforementioned lifeguard equipment buyer must first attempt to resell the equipment to a new buyer. Failure to satisfy the duty to mitigate will result in an inability to recover damages. In scenarios where damages are insufficient, a court may instead award specific performance . Under the specific performance remedy, the breaching party must attempt to fulfill the terms of the contract as best as possible. Specific performance, however, is generally only awarded when dealing with one-of-a-kind assets like real estate . Parties wishing to contract around the above remedies can do so through the use of liquidated damages provisions. These provisions establish in advance how much money a breaching party must pay and sidestep the expensive and time-consuming process of determining the actual damage caused by the breach. While liquidated damages clauses are generally allowed, a court may strike one down if the clause appears to be proxying for punitive damages or if the terms of the clause are unconscionable . perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity into D2's obligation to correct errors, weakening the original contractual obligation. The original text stated 'D2 shall promptly correct,' establishing a clear obligation. The modified text introduces terms like 'when feasible,' 'subject to resource availability,' and 'prioritization.' These phrases weaken the obligation, making it unclear when D2 is actually required to correct errors. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a breach of contract occurs when a party 'fails to perform their promised obligations.' The added conditions make it more difficult to determine if D2 has failed to perform because D2 can argue that correction was not feasible. This creates a situation where the obligation is significantly weakened and could be difficult to enforce, constituting a legal contradiction as the altered text undermines the original clear directive, and introduces conditions that could lead to non-performance of the original obligations. This aligns with the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: LICENSEE shall maintain records of its distribution of Licensee Products containing the Licensed Technology, for a period of one year after the date on which LICENSEE distributes the Products to which such records pertain.
perturbation.changed_text: LICENSEE will generally maintain records of its distribution of Licensee Products containing the Licensed Technology for approximately one year after the distribution date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain records' to 'will generally maintain records' significantly weakens the obligation. Replacing 'for a period of one year after the date' with 'for approximately one year after the distribution date' introduces further ambiguity and provides room for interpretation, making it unclear how precisely the licensee should keep the records. It can become difficult to enforce if the licensee fails to maintain the records for one year due to that vagueness.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text lacks the preciseness required by IRS regulations for maintaining records related to business operations. Section 6001 of the Internal Revenue Code necessitates the maintenance of adequate records to accurately determine tax liability. The ambiguous wording ('generally maintain', 'approximately one year') could be interpreted as failing to meet the standard of 'adequate records' required by the IRS, potentially leading to issues if the IRS audits and cannot verify the distribution of licensed products, impacting revenue calculations and royalty payments.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 CFR § 1.6001-1 - Records. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 26 CFR § 1.6001-1 - Records. CFR prev | next § 1.6001-1 Records. (a) In general. Except as provided in paragraph (b) of this section, any person subject to tax under subtitle A of the Code (including a qualified State individual income tax which is treated pursuant to section 6361(a) as if it were imposed by chapter 1 of subtitle A), or any person required to file a return of information with respect to income , shall keep such permanent books of account or records, including inventories , as are sufficient to establish the amount of gross income , deductions , credits, or other matters required to be shown by such person in any return of such tax or information. (b) Farmers and wage-earners. Individuals deriving gross income from the business of farming, and individuals whose gross income includes salaries, wages , or similar compensation for personal services rendered, are required with respect to such income to keep such records as will enable the district director to determine the correct amount of income subject to the tax. It is not necessary, however, that with respect to such income individuals keep the books of account or records required by paragraph (a) of this section. For rules with respect to the records to be kept in substantiation of traveling and other business expenses of employees , see § 1.162-17. (c) Exempt organizations. In addition to such permanent books and records as are required by paragraph (a) of this section with respect to the tax imposed by section 511 on unrelated business income of certain exempt organizations , every organization exempt from tax under section 501(a) shall keep such permanent books of account or records, including inventories , as are sufficient to show specifically the items of gross income , receipts and disbursements. Such organizations shall also keep such bo
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain records, creating ambiguity regarding the duration and consistency of record-keeping. While 26 USC 6001 states that 'Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title...shall keep such records', the changed text uses 'will generally maintain records for approximately one year,' which does not meet the standard for keeping adequate records required to determine tax liability. The original text used 'shall maintain...for a period of one year,' a clear directive absent in the changed text. This ambiguity undermines the IRS's ability to verify the distribution of licensed products, potentially impacting revenue calculations and royalty payments, as noted in the Law Explanation. The 'approximately' weakens the 'one year' requirement to the point that it contradicts the need for precise record keeping demanded by the IRS.
